description: Blog & Articles System - SEO-optimized guides explaining each agentic commerce category

userStories.title: Blog infrastructure & sitemap integration
userStories.description: As a developer, I need the foundational blog system so articles can be created, routed, and discovered by search engines and LLMs.
acceptanceCriteria: Create src/data/articles.ts with article type definition (slug, title, category, publishedDate, metaDescription, keywords, readingTime, content sections with heading/body pairs, faq array with question/answer pairs, relatedArticleSlugs)
Create /articles index page listing all articles with title, date, category badge, reading time, and excerpt
Create /articles/[slug] dynamic route for individual article pages
Article pages use semantic HTML: <article> wrapper, <time datetime=''> for dates, <section> for content blocks, proper H1 > H2 > H3 heading hierarchy, no heading level skips
Articles index page uses the same dark theme and header/footer as existing category pages
generateStaticParams exports all article slugs for static generation
Update src/app/sitemap.ts to include /articles (priority 0.6) and all /articles/[slug] pages (priority 0.7) — use each article's publishedDate as lastModified, not new Date()
Update src/app/feed.xml/route.ts RSS feed to include article entries with proper titles, links, descriptions, and pubDate using the article's publishedDate
Add 'Guides' link to the header navigation on all pages (homepage, category pages, article pages)

Create /llms.txt route that outputs a plain-text site overview for LLM crawlers: site name, purpose, list of all article URLs with titles, list of all category URLs — so LLMs can discover and understand the full site structure
Each article page includes a 'Related Articles' section at the bottom linking to 2-3 other articles (using relatedArticleSlugs from article data)
Each article page includes a link back to the corresponding /category/[slug] page
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: This is the foundation — must be done before any individual article stories. Articles are data-driven from src/data/articles.ts, not markdown files. Each article links back to its corresponding /category/[slug] page. The llms.txt file is an emerging standard — keep it simple plain text, not JSON.

userStories.title: Article SEO, structured data & server-side rendering
userStories.description: As a site owner, I need each article page to have proper SEO metadata, JSON-LD structured data, and SSR so search engines and LLM crawlers can fully index the content.
acceptanceCriteria: Each article page generates dynamic Metadata with unique title (format: '[Article Title] | Agentic Commerce Market Map'), description, canonical URL (/articles/[slug]), and openGraph/twitter tags

Each article page includes JSON-LD @type Article with: headline, datePublished, dateModified, author (@type Person), publisher (@type Organization with name and logo), description, articleSection (category name), keywords array, inLanguage 'en-US', mainEntityOfPage pointing to the canonical URL, image
Each article page includes BreadcrumbList JSON-LD (Home > Guides > Article Title)
Each article page includes FAQPage JSON-LD schema with the article's FAQ question/answer pairs (for featured snippets and LLM extraction)
Article content is fully server-rendered (no client-side fetching or useEffect for content) — all content in the initial HTML response
Meta description is unique per article and under 160 characters
OG image falls back to the site default or generates a dynamic one per article using src/app/articles/[slug]/opengraph-image.tsx
Articles index page has its own Metadata (title: 'Guides — Agentic Commerce Market Map', proper description, canonical URL)
robots.ts continues to allow all crawlers including AI bots (ChatGPT-User, ClaudeBot, GPTBot, anthropic-ai, cohere-ai, Google-Extended, etc.)
Typecheck passes
Verify structured data is valid by inspecting the rendered HTML — no missing required fields
userStories.notes: Critical for the SEO/LLMO goals. Every article must be a fully static, server-rendered page with rich structured data. No client components for article content. The FAQPage schema is essential for LLMs and Google featured snippets.

userStories.title: Article: Standards & Protocols for Agentic Commerce
userStories.description: As a reader, I want a detailed guide explaining what Standards & Protocols means in agentic commerce, why it matters, and how x402, ERC-8004, MCP, A2A, ACP, AP2, UCP, and MPP form the foundation of the agent economy.

Minimum 1500 words of substantive content split into clear H2/H3 sections — no heading level skips
Includes FAQ section with 3-5 common questions and concise answers (e.g., 'What is x402?', 'How do MCP and A2A differ?')
Includes relatedArticleSlugs linking to 2-3 other articles (e.g., payment-infrastructure-guide, payment-processors-guide)
Links back to /category/standards-and-protocols

Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: This is the foundational article — standards underpin everything else. Should explain how these protocols enable the entire ecosystem.

userStories.title: Article: How to Book Plane Tickets Today with an AI Agent Using OpenClaw & Virtual Debit Cards
userStories.description: As a reader, I want a practical, step-by-step guide showing how I can use OpenClaw with a virtual debit card (via Lobster.cash / CreditClaw) to have an AI agent autonomously search, compare, and book plane tickets for me — including how the virtual card system keeps my real payment info safe.
acceptanceCriteria: Article stored in src/data/articles.ts with slug 'book-flights-with-openclaw-agent'
Published date: 2026-03-19

Content covers: what OpenClaw is and how it runs locally on your device, the problem (giving an AI agent your credit card is dangerous — prompt injection, plaintext credentials), the solution: virtual debit cards with spending limits (Lobster.cash by Crossmint + Visa Intelligent Commerce + Solana USDC, CreditClaw, Privacy.com), step-by-step walkthrough of booking a flight (tell the agent your trip details, agent searches flights, compares prices, selects best option, uses scoped virtual card to complete booking, confirms with you), how the virtual card system works (agent never sees raw card details, requests scoped cards via Visa VIC or USDC, human sets spending limits and approves), security considerations (OpenClaw called 'insecure by default' by security analysts — virtual cards are the mitigation), what sites/airlines this works with today, limitations and what's coming next
Minimum 1500 words of substantive content split into clear H2/H3 sections — no heading level skips

Includes FAQ section with 3-5 common questions and concise answers (e.g., 'Is it safe to let an AI book flights for me?', 'What happens if the agent books the wrong flight?', 'How much does a virtual card cost?', 'Which airlines work with OpenClaw?')
Includes relatedArticleSlugs linking to 2-3 other articles (e.g., agent-harness-guide, wallets-and-tooling-guide, crypto-commerce-guide)
Links back to /category/agent-harness and /category/wallets-and-tooling
Mentions OpenClaw, Lobster.cash, CreditClaw, Crossmint, Visa, Privacy.com with context
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: This is a practical how-to article, not a category overview. Should feel like a tutorial that a non-technical person can follow. Emphasize safety — the virtual card is what makes this viable. Be honest about limitations and security risks.

acceptanceCriteria: Article stored in src/data/articles.ts with slug 'tempo-mainnet-launch-mpp'
Published date: 2026-03-19

Content covers: what Tempo is (payments-first L1 blockchain by Stripe and Paradigm, 100K+ TPS, sub-second finality), the mainnet launch on March 18 2026, what the Machine Payments Protocol (MPP) is (open standard co-authored with Stripe for agent-to-service payments — requesting, authorizing, and settling payments between machines), MPP key features (sessions for continuous payments, payment-method agnostic, HTTP-based control flow, payments directory with 100+ services at launch), MPP network extensions (Visa for card payments, Stripe for cards/wallets, Lightspark for Bitcoin Lightning), major launch partners (Anthropic, OpenAI, DoorDash, Mastercard, Shopify, Nubank, Revolut, Standard Chartered, Visa, UBS), use cases (pay-per-call APIs, monetized MCP servers, gated content, multi-service agent workflows), how Tempo compares to other chains in the agentic commerce stack (Base, Solana), what this means for the future of agent payments
Minimum 1500 words of substantive content split into clear H2/H3 sections — no heading level skips

Includes FAQ section with 3-5 common questions and concise answers (e.g., 'What is the Machine Payments Protocol?', 'How is Tempo different from Base or Solana?', 'Can my AI agent use Tempo today?', 'Who built Tempo?')
Includes relatedArticleSlugs linking to 2-3 other articles (e.g., blockchains-guide, payment-infrastructure-guide, standards-and-protocols-guide)
Links back to /category/blockchains and /category/standards-and-protocols and /category/payment-infrastructure
Mentions Tempo, Stripe, Paradigm, Visa, Mastercard, Anthropic, OpenAI, Shopify, Lightspark with context
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: This is a news/analysis article, not a category overview. Should feel timely and authoritative. Cover both the what (launch details) and the so-what (implications for the ecosystem). Reference that MPP specs are open-source on GitHub. This is a major event — Stripe entering blockchain with real launch partners signals mainstream adoption.
